news_summary: Recent space discoveries have advanced our understanding of black holes and revealed the presence of sugar molecules in interstellar space, shedding new light on cosmic phenomena and the origins of life.
news_summary_fr: Les récentes découvertes spatiales ont permis d'approfondir notre compréhension des trous noirs et ont révélé la présence de molécules de sucre dans l'espace interstellaire, apportant ainsi un nouvel éclairage sur les phénomènes cosmiques et les origines de la vie.
news_summary_es: Los recientes descubrimientos espaciales han ampliado nuestro conocimiento sobre los agujeros negros y han revelado la presencia de moléculas de azúcar en el espacio interestelar, lo que ha aportado nuevos datos sobre los fenómenos cósmicos y los orígenes de la vida.

articles.title: Stephen Hawking's black hole laws just got a major upgrade
articles.summary: Scientists have developed a new framework that could finally apply the laws of thermodynamics to real, ever-changing black holes instead of only perfectly stable ones. The advance may improve our understanding of black hole mergers, evaporation, and the powerful gravitational wave events detected by observatories like LIGO.
articles.content:
Scientists have proposed a new way to describe black holes that could overcome a major limitation in one of Stephen Hawking's most influential ideas. The research introduces an updated approach to black hole thermodynamics that works even when black holes are changing over time, potentially offering new insights into how they form, merge, and slowly evaporate.

Black holes are among the most extreme objects in the known universe. They squeeze enormous amounts of mass into an incredibly small region, creating gravity so intense that not even light can escape. To understand these cosmic objects, physicists rely on Einstein's theory of general relativity and quantum mechanics.

In the early 1970s, Stephen Hawking and other researchers discovered surprising connections between the laws of thermodynamics, which describe familiar processes such as heating water on a stove, and the behavior of black holes.

"Hawking's laws of black hole mechanics provided a satisfying connecting between extreme and ordinary physics and have been the paradigm for 50 years, but they have a serious limitation," said Abhay Ashtekar, Atherton University Professor and Evan Pugh Professor of Physics Emeritus in the Eberly College of Science at Penn State and the leader of the research team. "They were formulated for black holes at equilibrium, or unchanging over time, but black holes are constantly changing, they form, merge and eventually evaporate. We wanted to find a way to overcome this limitation and extend the laws to black holes that are out of equilibrium."

Ashtekar and his colleagues have now proposed a new method for determining a black hole's entropy, a quantity that measures disorder and, according to the second law of thermodynamics, can never decrease. Their findings, published in Physical Review Letters and selected as an Editor's Suggestion, introduce an entropy measure that is more closely connected to a black hole's spin and energy. The researchers say this could improve scientists' understanding of dynamic events such as black hole mergers and evaporation.

Why Hawking's Framework Needed an Update

"The laws of black hole mechanics came directly from Einstein's equations," said Daniel E. Paraizo, a graduate student in physics at Penn State and an author of the paper. "Because you cannot see into a black hole, it seemed that there could be an infinite number of ways to make a black hole making their entropy infinite as well. They were also thought to only absorb energy and never radiate, so their temperature was zero."

At first, those ideas made black holes appear incompatible with the familiar laws of thermodynamics because they seemed to have infinite entropy and no temperature. Hawking later changed that picture by using quantum mechanics to demonstrate that black holes can emit particles and energy.

"This changed the thinking about the thermodynamic properties black holes from a sort of mathematical concept described by equations, to being more of a physical reality," Paraizo said. "This opened the door to finding analogies in black holes of entropy and temperature used in thermodynamics."

Hawking proposed that the size of a black hole's event horizon, the boundary beyond which even light cannot escape, is proportional to its entropy. He also showed that a black hole's temperature depends on a combination of its mass and spin.

A Better Measure for Dynamic Black Holes

According to the researchers, the problem is that Hawking's approach works only when a black hole is in equilibrium.

"There is a problem, though," said Jonathan Shu, a graduate student in physics at Penn State and an author of the paper. "These analogies only really work for a black hole that is at equilibrium. In dynamic situations, event horizons can form and grow in what we call flat regions of space-time, where nothing is happening. This makes them teleological -- their properties cannot be determined just by the local physics of the black hole but instead rely on prediction of events that may or may not happen in the future. Therefore, the area of event horizons cannot be a measure of the physical entropy of dynamical black holes. If we want to understand black holes that are growing, evaporating, and merging, we need a viable alternative."

The team's solution replaces the traditional event horizon with what physicists call a "dynamical horizon," a concept that is already widely used in computer simulations of black holes. Unlike an event horizon, a dynamical horizon is defined by the black hole's properties at a specific moment in time, avoiding the complications created by relying on future events.

"This allows us to extend the first and second laws of thermodynamics to black holes that are not at equilibrium, thereby overcoming the limitations of the paradigm that has been used for over half a century," Ashtekar said. "We can apply these generalized laws to better understand evaporating black holes in quantum theory and black hole mergers, like those detected by the LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA collaboration using gravitational waves."

The research was supported by the Penn State Atherton Professorship Program and the Penn State Eberly College of Science.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough that updates Stephen Hawking's black hole thermodynamics laws to apply to dynamic black holes, overcoming a major limitation and potentially improving understanding of black hole mergers, evaporation, and gravitational wave events. This advance has broad implications for astrophysics and quantum theory, impacting the general scientific community and enhancing knowledge about the universe.
articles.personality_title: Scientists update Stephen Hawking’s black hole laws for changing black holes
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Black holes are mysterious objects in space with gravity so strong that nothing can escape them. In the 1970s, Stephen Hawking helped connect the way black holes work with the laws of thermodynamics, which explain heat and energy. But his ideas only worked for black holes that stay the same over time, not for those that change.

**What happened** – A team of scientists led by Abhay Ashtekar at Penn State has created a new way to describe black holes that change over time. Instead of using the traditional idea of an event horizon, they use something called a "dynamical horizon" that looks at a black hole’s properties at a specific moment. This allows the laws of thermodynamics to apply to black holes that grow, merge, or shrink.

**Impact** – This new method solves an important problem because black holes in space are always changing. It can help scientists better understand events like black hole mergers and how black holes slowly evaporate. It also improves the study of gravitational waves, which are ripples in space detected by observatories like LIGO.

**What's next step** – Researchers will use this updated framework to study real black hole events and test how well it matches observations. This could lead to new discoveries about how black holes behave and their role in the universe.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have improved Stephen Hawking’s black hole laws so they now work for black holes that change, helping us understand these cosmic giants better.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les lois des trous noirs de Stephen Hawking mises à jour pour les trous noirs changeants
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les trous noirs sont des objets mystérieux dans l’espace avec une gravité si forte que rien ne peut en échapper. Dans les années 1970, Stephen Hawking a relié le fonctionnement des trous noirs aux lois de la thermodynamique, qui expliquent la chaleur et l’énergie. Mais ses idées ne fonctionnaient que pour les trous noirs stables, pas pour ceux qui changent.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de scientifiques dirigée par Abhay Ashtekar à Penn State a créé une nouvelle façon de décrire les trous noirs qui évoluent dans le temps. Au lieu d’utiliser l’idée traditionnelle de l’horizon des événements, ils utilisent un « horizon dynamique » qui regarde les propriétés d’un trou noir à un moment précis. Cela permet d’appliquer les lois de la thermodynamique aux trous noirs qui grandissent, fusionnent ou rétrécissent.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle méthode résout un problème important car les trous noirs changent toujours dans l’espace. Elle peut aider les scientifiques à mieux comprendre des événements comme les fusions de trous noirs et leur évaporation lente. Cela améliore aussi l’étude des ondes gravitationnelles, des ondulations dans l’espace détectées par des observatoires comme LIGO.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont utiliser ce cadre mis à jour pour étudier de vrais événements de trous noirs et vérifier son exactitude avec les observations. Cela pourrait mener à de nouvelles découvertes sur le comportement des trous noirs et leur rôle dans l’univers.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont amélioré les lois de Stephen Hawking pour qu’elles fonctionnent aussi avec les trous noirs changeants, ce qui nous aide à mieux comprendre ces géants cosmiques.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos actualizan las leyes de Stephen Hawking para agujeros negros cambiantes
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los agujeros negros son objetos misteriosos en el espacio con una gravedad tan fuerte que nada puede escapar de ellos. En los años 70, Stephen Hawking relacionó cómo funcionan los agujeros negros con las leyes de la termodinámica, que explican el calor y la energía. Pero sus ideas solo funcionaban para agujeros negros que permanecen iguales, no para los que cambian.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de científicos liderado por Abhay Ashtekar en Penn State creó una nueva forma de describir los agujeros negros que cambian con el tiempo. En lugar de usar la idea tradicional del horizonte de eventos, usan un "horizonte dinámico" que observa las propiedades de un agujero negro en un momento específico. Esto permite que las leyes de la termodinámica se apliquen a agujeros negros que crecen, se unen o se encogen.

**Impacto** – Este nuevo método resuelve un problema importante porque los agujeros negros siempre están cambiando en el espacio. Puede ayudar a los científicos a entender mejor eventos como las fusiones de agujeros negros y cómo se evaporan lentamente. También mejora el estudio de las ondas gravitacionales, que son ondas en el espacio detectadas por observatorios como LIGO.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores usarán este marco actualizado para estudiar eventos reales de agujeros negros y comprobar qué tan bien coincide con las observaciones. Esto podría llevar a nuevos descubrimientos sobre cómo se comportan los agujeros negros y su papel en el universo.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los científicos han mejorado las leyes de Stephen Hawking para que funcionen con agujeros negros que cambian, ayudándonos a entender mejor a estos gigantes cósmicos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a swirling, evolving black hole represented as a glowing, spinning vortex of light and energy, surrounded by a softly illuminated, shifting boundary symbolizing the new “dynamical horizon,” with subtle interwoven threads of golden and silver light suggesting the flow of entropy and thermodynamic laws in motion.

articles.title: First ‘true sugar’ molecule found in space — offering hints to life’s origins
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 13 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02173-5Erythrulose, which has four carbon atoms, is the most complex sugar spotted beyond our Solar System.
articles.content:
Researchers found a four-carbon sugar called erythrulose in a cloud of gas and dust at the centre of the Milky Way (image from Spitzer Space Telescope shown here).Credit: NASA, Caltech, Susan Stolovy (SSC, Caltech)

Call it an extra-sweet discovery. A group of astronomers has detected a sugar molecule swirling inside a cloud of gas and dust near the centre of our galaxy. They are calling the molecule — a compound with four carbon atoms called erythrulose — the first true sugar spotted in ‘interstellar’ space. The findings, reported today in the journal Nature Astronomy1, could help clarify how life on Earth began.

Asteroid fragments upend theory of how life on Earth bloomed

“This is an incredibly exciting result,” says Brett McGuire, an astrochemist at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology in Cambridge. “Astronomers have, for a very long time, been pushing to detect sugars in space.”

That’s because they’ve already seen hints that sugars on Earth that are essential to life originated in outer space. For instance, the five-carbon sugar ribose was previously found in some multi-billion-year-old meteorite samples2,3, suggesting that space rocks might have smashed into Earth and delivered this and other sugars.

In 2000, astronomers reported the detection of the two-carbon molecule glycolaldehyde — sometimes considered the simplest sugar molecule — in the interstellar space between stars4. But although glycolaldehyde can act similarly to a sugar, “it is not formally a sugar”, McGuire says. True sugars, he adds, must have a backbone of at least three carbon atoms. Astronomers have continued to scan space for these bigger molecules ever since.

A surprise signature

Izaskun Jiménez-Serra, an astronomer at the Spanish National Research Council in Madrid, and her colleagues were among this group, but hadn’t had any luck while scouring the night sky. Then, in 2022, Emilio Cocinero, a physical chemist at the University of the Basque Country in Leioa, Spain, offered to share spectroscopic data for erythrulose — that is, the specific fingerprint of wavelengths that the molecule emits.

Erythrulose has four carbons (grey). Red atoms are oxygens, and white atoms are hydrogens.Source: Ref. 1

“I said, ‘Okay, why don’t you send me the information, and then I’ll check whether we see it in our data’,” Jiménez-Serra says. She was sceptical at first. But to her surprise, the erythrulose fingerprint showed up in the team’s observations of a molecular cloud near the centre of the Milky Way. And using Spain’s Yebes 40-metre and IRAM 30-metre radio telescopes for deeper observations, Jiménez-Serra and her team saw the signal even more clearly.

Feedstock for life?
articles.reasoning: The discovery of the first true sugar molecule (erythrulose) in interstellar space is a significant scientific breakthrough that advances our understanding of the origins of life. It has broad implications for astrobiology and the study of life's building blocks beyond Earth, representing a meaningful and inspiring advancement in space science.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find first true sugar molecule in space near Milky Way center
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long searched for sugars in space because these molecules are important for life on Earth. Some simpler sugar-like molecules were found before, but no true sugar with three or more carbon atoms had been detected outside our Solar System.

**What happened** – In 2026, a group of astronomers discovered a sugar molecule called erythrulose in a cloud of gas and dust near the center of the Milky Way galaxy. Erythrulose has four carbon atoms, making it the first true sugar found in interstellar space. The discovery was made using radio telescopes in Spain, which detected the unique signals or “fingerprints” of this molecule.

**Impact** – This finding is important because sugars are key building blocks for life, such as in DNA and RNA. Finding erythrulose in space suggests that some ingredients for life might form in space and travel to planets like Earth. This helps scientists understand how life’s essential molecules could have arrived on our planet long ago.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will continue to study other complex molecules in space and search for more sugars or life-related chemicals. They will also use better telescopes to explore more regions of our galaxy and learn how these molecules form and move through space.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, scientists have found a true sugar molecule in space, offering new clues about how life’s building blocks may form beyond Earth.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent la première vraie molécule de sucre dans l’espace près du centre de la Voie lactée
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchaient depuis longtemps des sucres dans l’espace car ces molécules sont importantes pour la vie sur Terre. Certains composés ressemblant à des sucres plus simples avaient été trouvés, mais aucune vraie molécule de sucre avec trois atomes de carbone ou plus n’avait été détectée hors de notre système solaire.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2026, un groupe d’astronomes a découvert une molécule de sucre appelée érythrulose dans un nuage de gaz et de poussière près du centre de la galaxie de la Voie lactée. L’érythrulose contient quatre atomes de carbone, ce qui en fait le premier vrai sucre trouvé dans l’espace interstellaire. Cette découverte a été réalisée grâce à des radiotélescopes en Espagne, qui ont détecté les signaux uniques de cette molécule.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car les sucres sont des éléments clés pour la vie, notamment dans l’ADN et l’ARN. Trouver de l’érythrulose dans l’espace suggère que certains ingrédients de la vie peuvent se former dans l’espace et voyager jusqu’à des planètes comme la Terre. Cela aide les scientifiques à comprendre comment les molécules essentielles à la vie ont pu arriver sur notre planète il y a très longtemps.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à étudier d’autres molécules complexes dans l’espace et chercher plus de sucres ou de produits chimiques liés à la vie. Ils utiliseront aussi de meilleurs télescopes pour explorer d’autres régions de notre galaxie et comprendre comment ces molécules se forment et se déplacent dans l’espace.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Pour la première fois, des scientifiques ont trouvé une vraie molécule de sucre dans l’espace, donnant de nouveaux indices sur la formation des éléments essentiels à la vie au-delà de la Terre.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos encuentran la primera molécula verdadera de azúcar en el espacio cerca del centro de la Vía Láctea
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han buscado durante mucho tiempo azúcares en el espacio porque estas moléculas son importantes para la vida en la Tierra. Se habían encontrado antes moléculas similares a azúcares más simples, pero nunca un azúcar verdadero con tres o más átomos de carbono fuera de nuestro Sistema Solar.

**Qué pasó** – En 2026, un grupo de astrónomos descubrió una molécula de azúcar llamada eritroluosa en una nube de gas y polvo cerca del centro de la galaxia Vía Láctea. La eritroluosa tiene cuatro átomos de carbono, siendo el primer azúcar verdadero encontrado en el espacio interestelar. El hallazgo se realizó usando telescopios de radio en España que detectaron las señales únicas de esta molécula.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque los azúcares son bloques básicos para la vida, como en el ADN y ARN. Encontrar eritroluosa en el espacio sugiere que algunos ingredientes para la vida pueden formarse en el espacio y viajar a planetas como la Tierra. Esto ayuda a los científicos a entender cómo las moléculas esenciales para la vida pudieron llegar a nuestro planeta hace mucho tiempo.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando otras moléculas complejas en el espacio y buscando más azúcares o químicos relacionados con la vida. También usarán mejores telescopios para explorar más regiones de nuestra galaxia y aprender cómo se forman y se mueven estas moléculas en el espacio.

**Resumen en una frase** – Por primera vez, los científicos han encontrado una molécula verdadera de azúcar en el espacio, ofreciendo nuevas pistas sobre cómo se forman los bloques esenciales de la vida fuera de la Tierra.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing, intricately structured four-carbon sugar molecule (erythrulose) composed of grey carbon spheres, red oxygen spheres, and white hydrogen spheres, floating gently within a softly illuminated swirling cloud of gas and dust at the Milky Way’s center, with subtle radio telescope dishes observing from below under a starry night sky rendered in natural, muted tones.

articles.title: NASA’s Hubble Discovers First of Star Cluster’s Missing Black Holes
articles.summary: Astronomers using archival data from Hubble and supportive observations from Webb have located their first stellar-mass black hole in the star cluster Omega Centauri.
articles.content:
The massive globular star cluster Omega Centauri has puzzled astronomers for decades. It should be filled with black holes left behind by exploding stars, yet evidence for them is scarce. Now, astronomers using archival data from NASA’s Hubble Space Telescope and supportive observations from NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope have finally located their first stellar-mass black hole in this cluster. Discovering the first of this missing black hole population will help refine current theories on black hole formation within environments such as Omega Centauri. The team’s findings published Monday in The Astrophysical Journal Letters.

Omega Centauri is composed of 10 million gravitationally bound stars. Though the astronomical community previously found evidence with Hubble that an intermediate-mass black hole lurks at its center, models suggest this star cluster should also contain about 10,000 smaller, stellar-mass black holes. This notable population of black holes evaded detection in previous observational studies, which used the radial velocity method or looked for radio and X-ray emission from material falling onto black holes.

This new discovery features a different approach, known as astrometry, to measure very small movements of stars over time. By sifting through more than 20 years of Hubble archival data and pulling in recent Webb data to further refine their astrometric measurements, the team located a star orbiting an invisible object so hefty that it has to be a black hole. Dubbed oMEGACat BH-2, it is the first stellar-mass black hole detected in Omega Centauri, and it has some surprising qualities. oMEGACat BH-2 has a lower-than-expected mass and, with its visible star companion, the black hole-star duo has the longest orbital period of any black hole binary system known to date.

“With Hubble and Webb data, we were able to see the motion of the visible main sequence star that is part of this binary, which is about 18,000 light-years away in the dense environment of Omega Centauri,” said Matthew Whitaker of the University of Utah, Salt Lake City, lead author of the paper. “The precision of these measurements is incredible, down to a fraction of a pixel on Hubble and Webb’s detectors. It would not have been possible to find this black hole without these two space telescopes.”

Astronomers found Omega Centauri’s first stellar-mass black hole, which has a visible star companion that is shown in greater detail. They used 20-plus years of data from NASA’s Hubble Space Telescope and recent data from NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope to make the discovery. Image: ESA, NASA, Maximilian Häberle (MPIA), Joseph DePasquale (STScI)

The team’s findings refine a past study by a different group of scientists that suggested this binary system included a neutron star. By expanding Hubble data from the earlier investigation with archival Hubble astrometric measurements from 2002 to 2023, and pulling in Webb near-infrared data to improve precision, the University of Utah-led team was able to better constrain the mass of the visible star’s dark companion, ruling out the neutron star possibility.

“While we already knew that the star was 0.78 solar masses, we can now calculate the black hole’s mass, which is 4.46 solar masses and therefore too heavy to be a neutron star. However, its mass is much lower than would be expected in a metal-poor environment like Omega Centauri. This is surprising and exciting,” said Anil Seth of the University of Utah, a coauthor of the study. “We now know that a metal-poor star is able to form a black hole like this, and we need to figure out how that happens. This detection is providing some data to those who do that kind of modeling.”

Long time coming

Based on the precise data from Hubble and Webb, the team could chart the star’s path over 20-plus years, during its closest approach to its black hole companion when it moved the fastest across the sky. From the extensive data, the team determined that the visible star orbits oMEGACat BH-2 once every 94 years, making it the longest-period black hole binary ever known.

Its long orbital period also gives a clue to the origin of this binary system. It was probably dynamically formed, meaning the star and its black hole companion did not start out together but rather found each other in this cluster. The researchers calculated that a system like oMEGACat BH-2 will survive for less than a billion years before it is torn apart by encounters with nearby stars, a much shorter span than the age of the cluster (approximately 12 billion years old).

“It's important to understand black hole populations in globular clusters because there's uncertainty about their physics and formation,” said Seth. “More specifically, understanding the process of forming black holes and then dynamically forming binaries is vital, because it affects our ability to interpret and understand gravitational wave events. Environments like Omega Centauri are the primary places where we think binaries are merging and creating these waves.”

The team’s discovery of stellar-mass black hole oMEGACat BH-2 with the Hubble-Webb dataset is just the start of finding these evasive black hole populations in globular star clusters.

“With Hubble and Webb, we can continue to look at Omega Centauri and expand our search for similar systems within other clusters,” said Whitaker. “We’re also very excited for the launch of NASA’s Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope because it will image the crowded galactic bulge, including the galactic center, very regularly with Hubble-like resolution and with a much wider field of view. We’re hoping we’ll be able to find black hole binary systems like this one because of the regular cadence of Roman’s observations.”

The Hubble Space Telescope has been operating for over three decades and continues to make ground-breaking discoveries that shape our fundamental understanding of the universe. Hubble is a project of international cooperation between NASA and ESA (European Space Agency). NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center in Greenbelt, Maryland, manages the telescope and mission operations. Lockheed Martin Space, based in Denver, also supports mission operations at Goddard. The Space Telescope Science Institute in Baltimore, which is operated by the Association of Universities for Research in Astronomy, conducts Hubble science operations for NASA.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of the first stellar-mass black hole in the Omega Centauri star cluster using data from Hubble and Webb telescopes. This discovery advances understanding of black hole formation and dynamics in globular clusters, which has broad implications for astrophysics and gravitational wave research. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a meaningful breakthrough with wide scientific and public interest.
articles.personality_title: Hubble and Webb find first small black hole in Omega Centauri star cluster
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Omega Centauri is a huge group of about 10 million stars held together by gravity. Scientists expected to find many black holes there, but until now, none had been clearly spotted. Black holes are objects with gravity so strong that not even light can escape. 

**What happened** – Using over 20 years of data from NASA’s Hubble Space Telescope and recent observations from the James Webb Space Telescope, astronomers found the first small black hole in Omega Centauri. They noticed a star moving in a way that showed it was orbiting something invisible and very heavy—a black hole named oMEGACat BH-2. This black hole has a star companion, and they orbit each other every 94 years, the longest orbit seen in such a system.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it confirms that small black holes exist in Omega Centauri, as scientists predicted but had not found before. The black hole’s mass is smaller than expected for this kind of star cluster, which challenges current ideas about how black holes form in environments with few heavy elements. Also, finding a binary system with such a long orbit helps scientists understand how stars and black holes come together in dense star groups.

**What’s next step** – Scientists plan to keep looking for more black holes in Omega Centauri and other star clusters using Hubble, Webb, and soon NASA’s new Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope. The Roman telescope will take regular images over large areas of space, making it easier to spot more black hole pairs. This will help improve knowledge about black holes and their role in producing gravitational waves—ripples in space caused by massive objects moving or merging.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, astronomers have found a small black hole in the Omega Centauri star cluster, opening new paths to study black hole formation and behavior in crowded star groups.
articles.personality_title_fr: Hubble et Webb découvrent le premier petit trou noir dans l’amas d’étoiles Omega Centauri
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Omega Centauri est un immense groupe d’environ 10 millions d’étoiles liées par la gravité. Les scientifiques s’attendaient à y trouver de nombreux trous noirs, mais aucun n’avait encore été clairement détecté. Un trou noir est un objet dont la gravité est si forte que même la lumière ne peut s’en échapper.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En utilisant plus de 20 ans de données du télescope spatial Hubble et des observations récentes du télescope spatial James Webb, des astronomes ont découvert le premier petit trou noir dans Omega Centauri. Ils ont observé une étoile se déplaçant comme si elle tournait autour d’un objet invisible et très massif : un trou noir nommé oMEGACat BH-2. Ce trou noir a une étoile compagne, et elles tournent l’une autour de l’autre tous les 94 ans, la plus longue période orbitale connue pour un tel système.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle confirme que des petits trous noirs existent dans Omega Centauri, comme les scientifiques le supposaient mais n’avaient jamais trouvé. La masse de ce trou noir est plus faible que prévu pour ce type d’amas, ce qui remet en question les idées actuelles sur la formation des trous noirs dans des environnements pauvres en éléments lourds. De plus, trouver un système binaire avec une orbite aussi longue aide à mieux comprendre comment étoiles et trous noirs se forment ensemble dans des groupes denses.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques prévoient de continuer à chercher d’autres trous noirs dans Omega Centauri et dans d’autres amas stellaires avec Hubble, Webb, et bientôt le nouveau télescope spatial Nancy Grace Roman de la NASA. Ce télescope prendra régulièrement des images sur de grandes zones du ciel, facilitant la détection de nouvelles paires de trous noirs. Cela améliorera la compréhension des trous noirs et de leur rôle dans la production d’ondes gravitationnelles, des ondulations dans l’espace causées par le mouvement ou la fusion d’objets massifs.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Pour la première fois, des astronomes ont découvert un petit trou noir dans l’amas d’étoiles Omega Centauri, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour étudier la formation et le comportement des trous noirs dans des groupes denses d’étoiles.
articles.personality_title_es: Hubble y Webb encuentran el primer agujero negro pequeño en el cúmulo estelar Omega Centauri
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Omega Centauri es un enorme grupo de aproximadamente 10 millones de estrellas unidas por la gravedad. Los científicos esperaban encontrar muchos agujeros negros allí, pero hasta ahora no habían detectado ninguno claramente. Un agujero negro es un objeto con gravedad tan fuerte que ni la luz puede escapar.

**Qué pasó** – Usando más de 20 años de datos del telescopio espacial Hubble y observaciones recientes del telescopio espacial James Webb, los astrónomos encontraron el primer agujero negro pequeño en Omega Centauri. Notaron una estrella moviéndose de forma que mostraba que orbitaba algo invisible y muy pesado: un agujero negro llamado oMEGACat BH-2. Este agujero negro tiene una estrella compañera, y orbitan uno alrededor del otro cada 94 años, el período orbital más largo conocido en un sistema así.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque confirma que los agujeros negros pequeños existen en Omega Centauri, como los científicos habían predicho pero no habían encontrado antes. La masa del agujero negro es menor de lo esperado para este tipo de cúmulo, lo que desafía las ideas actuales sobre cómo se forman los agujeros negros en ambientes con pocos elementos pesados. Además, encontrar un sistema binario con un órbita tan larga ayuda a entender cómo las estrellas y los agujeros negros se juntan en grupos de estrellas densos.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean seguir buscando más agujeros negros en Omega Centauri y en otros cúmulos estelares usando Hubble, Webb y pronto el nuevo Telescopio Espacial Nancy Grace Roman de la NASA. El telescopio Roman tomará imágenes regulares de grandes áreas del cielo, facilitando encontrar más pares de agujeros negros. Esto ayudará a mejorar el conocimiento sobre los agujeros negros y su papel en la creación de ondas gravitacionales, ondas en el espacio causadas por objetos masivos en movimiento o fusión.

**Resumen en una frase** – Por primera vez, los astrónomos han encontrado un agujero negro pequeño en el cúmulo estelar Omega Centauri, abriendo nuevas formas de estudiar la formación y comportamiento de los agujeros negros en grupos densos de estrellas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a vast, glowing globular star cluster with millions of stars gently swirling around a subtle, dark, spherical void at its center, symbolizing the newly discovered stellar-mass black hole; nearby, a single bright star orbits this dark sphere in a wide, slow ellipse, all rendered in warm, natural tones of deep blues, soft golds, and muted whites, evoking a sense of cosmic mystery and discovery.

articles.title: Sugar molecules found in interstellar space for the first time
articles.summary: Researchers have long suspected early life may have been helped by sugars brought to Earth by asteroids – now a sugar found in raspberries has been spotted in a cosmic cloud nearly 27 light years away
articles.content:
Researchers have long suspected early life may have been helped by sugars brought to Earth by asteroids – now a sugar found in raspberries has been spotted in a cosmic cloud nearly 27 light years away

Is there any sugar out there? NASA, JPL-Caltech, Susan Stolovy (SSC/Caltech) et al.

Sugar molecules have been found in interstellar space for the first time. The finding adds further evidence to the idea that early life on Earth may have benefited from complex sugar molecules arriving from elsewhere.

Sugars arise from chemical bonds among carbon, hydrogen and oxygen. Besides serving as an energy source, certain sugars are fundamental building blocks of RNA and DNA, the genetic material found in every living cell. However, there has long been a chicken-and-egg problem: living organisms make sugars with enzymes, but experiments recreating conditions on pre-life Earth have yielded relatively few.

Recent discoveries of sugars in meteorite and asteroid samples suggest that such bodies may have delivered them to the young Earth. Izaskun Jiménez-Serra at Centre for Astrobiology in Madrid, Spain, and her colleagues wondered if sugars might be present in interstellar space itself.

Advertisement

To find out, they aimed radio telescopes at G+0.693−0.027, a large molecular cloud 26,700 light years away already known to contain dozens of organic molecules, including glycolaldehyde, a compound involved in sugar formation but not considered a true sugar itself.

The team compared the cloud’s radio emissions with laboratory measurements of spectral fingerprints – distinctive patterns of radio frequencies that molecules absorb and emit – for the simplest sugars, glyceraldehyde and dihydroxyacetone.

When results came up empty for either of these three-carbon sugars, Jiménez-Serra says she decided to check for a more complex four-carbon sugar, erythrulose, one of the sugars found in raspberries. To her surprise, the team found an incredibly rich supply of the molecule.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Weekly Sign up to newsletter

“When I started looking at the signals, I was like, ‘I can’t believe this!’” she says. “This is really getting detected! And it’s a huge amount of sugar.”

The findings suggest that erythrulose may have formed before the solar system itself, become trapped inside asteroids and comets as they formed and finally reached the young Earth during the Late Heavy Bombardment about 4 billion years ago. This process could have delivered the equivalent of 500,000 to 50 million tonnes of erythrulose to the young Earth, says Jiménez-Serra.

“These molecular clouds are basically the birth sites of stars and planets,” she says. “So essentially, we’re finding that these complex organic compounds that are important for the origin of life can form already very early in the formation process of a planetary system.”

“This is a very exciting finding,” says Yoshihiro Furukawa at Tohoku University in Japan. “It points to an additional source of sugar distribution beyond asteroids. We have been waiting for an actual detection like this.”

Chemistry unlike anything naturally seen on Earth – playing out on icy dust grains chilled to around -250°C and bombarded by intense radiation – may have produced some of the molecular building blocks that ultimately gave rise to life, says Niels Ligterink at TU Delft in the Netherlands. “We can now say that the ingredients for biochemistry are likely spread throughout the galaxy,” he says.
articles.reasoning: The discovery of sugar molecules in interstellar space provides significant evidence supporting theories about the origins of life on Earth, showing that complex organic compounds essential for life can form early in planetary system formation. This finding has broad scientific and societal implications for understanding life's cosmic origins and the potential distribution of life's building blocks throughout the galaxy.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find sugar molecules in space for the first time
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have wondered if sugar molecules, important for life, could come from space. Sugars help build DNA and RNA, but it was unclear if they could form outside living cells. Some sugars were found in meteorites, hinting they might have arrived on Earth from space.

**What happened** – A team of researchers used radio telescopes to study a distant cloud in space, about 27,000 light years away, known to have many organic molecules. They searched for sugar molecules and found a four-carbon sugar called erythrulose, which is also found in raspberries. This was the first time sugar molecules were detected in interstellar space.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that sugars can form in space before planets and stars exist. It supports the idea that early Earth may have received large amounts of sugar from asteroids and comets, which could have helped life begin. It also means the building blocks of life are more common in the galaxy than we thought.

**What's next step** – Scientists will continue studying other space clouds to find more complex molecules. This research may help us understand how life’s ingredients spread across the galaxy and how life might start on other planets.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, sugar molecules have been found in space, showing that life’s essential ingredients can form far beyond Earth.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des molécules de sucre découvertes dans l’espace pour la première fois
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques se demandaient si des molécules de sucre, importantes pour la vie, pouvaient venir de l’espace. Les sucres aident à construire l’ADN et l’ARN, mais on ne savait pas s’ils pouvaient se former en dehors des cellules vivantes. Certains sucres ont été trouvés dans des météorites, suggérant qu’ils pourraient être arrivés sur Terre depuis l’espace.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de chercheurs a utilisé des radiotélescopes pour étudier un nuage lointain dans l’espace, à environ 27 000 années-lumière, connu pour contenir de nombreuses molécules organiques. Ils ont cherché des molécules de sucre et ont trouvé un sucre à quatre carbones appelé érythrulose, que l’on trouve aussi dans les framboises. C’est la première fois que des molécules de sucre sont détectées dans l’espace interstellaire.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que les sucres peuvent se former dans l’espace avant l’existence des planètes et des étoiles. Elle soutient l’idée que la Terre primitive a pu recevoir de grandes quantités de sucre par des astéroïdes et des comètes, ce qui aurait pu aider à l’apparition de la vie. Cela signifie aussi que les éléments de base de la vie sont plus répandus dans la galaxie que ce que l’on pensait.

**Étape suivante** – Les scientifiques vont continuer à étudier d’autres nuages spatiaux pour trouver des molécules plus complexes. Cette recherche pourrait aider à comprendre comment les ingrédients de la vie se dispersent dans la galaxie et comment la vie pourrait commencer sur d’autres planètes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Pour la première fois, des molécules de sucre ont été trouvées dans l’espace, montrant que les ingrédients essentiels à la vie peuvent se former bien au-delà de la Terre.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos encuentran moléculas de azúcar en el espacio por primera vez
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos se preguntaban si las moléculas de azúcar, importantes para la vida, podían venir del espacio. Los azúcares ayudan a construir el ADN y ARN, pero no estaba claro si podían formarse fuera de las células vivas. Algunos azúcares se encontraron en meteoritos, lo que sugiere que podrían haber llegado a la Tierra desde el espacio.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de investigadores usó radiotelescopios para estudiar una nube distante en el espacio, a unos 27,000 años luz, conocida por tener muchas moléculas orgánicas. Buscaron moléculas de azúcar y encontraron un azúcar de cuatro carbonos llamado eritrosulosa, que también se encuentra en las frambuesas. Esta es la primera vez que se detectan moléculas de azúcar en el espacio interestelar.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra que los azúcares pueden formarse en el espacio antes de que existan planetas y estrellas. Apoya la idea de que la Tierra primitiva pudo haber recibido grandes cantidades de azúcar de asteroides y cometas, lo que podría haber ayudado al inicio de la vida. También significa que los bloques básicos de la vida son más comunes en la galaxia de lo que se pensaba.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán estudiando otras nubes espaciales para encontrar moléculas más complejas. Esta investigación puede ayudar a entender cómo se distribuyen los ingredientes de la vida en la galaxia y cómo podría comenzar la vida en otros planetas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Por primera vez, se han encontrado moléculas de azúcar en el espacio, mostrando que los ingredientes esenciales para la vida pueden formarse mucho más allá de la Tierra.
articles.image_prompt: A softly glowing cosmic cloud filled with intricate, delicate molecular structures shaped like sugar crystals gently floating amid twinkling stars, with a subtle raspberry-shaped cluster of four-carbon sugar molecules illuminated at the center, all rendered in warm, natural hues of deep blues, soft purples, and gentle reds, evoking the birth of life's building blocks in interstellar space.
